source_paper_title: Combinatorial Auctions with Interdependent Valuations: SOS to the Rescue
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Eden_2024, title={Combinatorial Auctions with Interdependent Valuations: SOS to the Rescue}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1371}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1371}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Eden, Alon and Feldman, Michal and Fiat, Amos and Goldner, Kira and Karlin, Anna R.}, year={2024}, month=may, pages={653–674} }
context:
Section 7 (problems), page 16 (in the provided PDF pagination)

For combinatorial auctions with multidimensional signals, is separability a necessary condition for achieving constant approximation to welfare? This problem is open even for single-dimensional signals and even for “simple” combinatorial valuations, such as unit demand.
title: Constant-factor welfare approximation without separability in multidimensional-signal combinatorial auctions
background_latex:
Consider a combinatorial auction with a set of agents  \( [n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\) and a set of items \( [m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\). An allocation is a partition of items into disjoint bundles \((T_1,\dots,T_n)\) with \(T_i\subseteq [m]\) and \(T_i\cap T_{i'}=\emptyset\) for \(i\neq i'\); agent \(i\) receives bundle \(T_i\) (possibly \(\emptyset\)).

Signals are multidimensional over bundles: for every agent \(j\in[n]\) and every bundle \(T\subseteq [m]\), agent \(j\) has a private signal \(s_{jT}\in\mathbb{R}_+\). For each bundle \(T\subseteq[m]\), let \(s_T=(s_{1T},\dots,s_{nT})\in\mathbb{R}_+^n\). The full signal profile is \(s=\{s_T\}_{T\subseteq[m]}\).

Valuations are interdependent: for each agent \(i\in[n]\) and bundle \(T\subseteq[m]\), the value of agent \(i\) for receiving bundle \(T\) is a known function \(v_{iT}(s_T)\in\mathbb{R}_+\) of the entire bundle-specific signal vector \(s_T\). Each \(v_{iT}\) is assumed (as in the paper’s model) to be weakly increasing in every coordinate of \(s_T\) and strictly increasing in the agent’s own signal coordinate \(s_{iT}\).

Incentives: a (possibly randomized) direct-revelation mechanism maps reported signals to an allocation and payments. The incentive notion of interest is universal ex post incentive compatibility with ex post individual rationality (universally ex post IC-IR): the mechanism is a distribution over deterministic mechanisms, each of which makes truthful reporting an ex post Nash equilibrium and guarantees nonnegative utility to truthful agents.

Objective: social welfare at signal profile \(s\) under allocation \((T_1,\dots,T_n)\) is \(\sum_{i=1}^n v_{iT_i}(s_{T_i})\). The optimal welfare is the maximum of this quantity over all feasible allocations.

Valuation structure: the paper focuses on the class of valuations that are submodular over signals (SOS). Informally, for each \(v_{iT}\), the marginal increase from raising a single coordinate \(s_{jT}\) is weakly larger when the other coordinates of \(s_T\) are smaller (a diminishing-returns property on \(\mathbb{R}_+^n\)). The paper obtains constant-factor welfare approximations in the multidimensional-signal model only under an additional structural assumption called separability, where \(v_{iT}(s_T)\) can be decomposed as a sum of a term depending only on other agents’ signals and a term depending only on \(i\)’s own signal.

The open question is whether such separability is actually required for constant-factor approximation in the multidimensional-signal combinatorial-auction model under SOS valuations.
problem_definition_latex: Determine whether there exists a universally ex post IC-IR mechanism \(\mathcal{M}\) and a universal constant \(c\ge 1\) such that, for every instance of a combinatorial auction with multidimensional bundle-specific signals \(s=\{s_T\}_{T\subseteq[m]}\) and SOS valuation functions \(\{v_{iT}:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}_+\}_{i\in[n],\,T\subseteq[m]}\) (with no separability assumption), the expected welfare achieved by \(\mathcal{M}\) at every signal profile \(s\) is at least \(\frac{1}{c}\) times the optimal welfare at \(s\).

Determine whether there exists a universally ex post IC-IR mechanism \(\mathcal{M}\) and a universal constant \(c\ge 1\) such that, for every instance of a combinatorial auction with multidimensional bundle-specific signals \(s=\{s_T\}_{T\subseteq[m]}\) and SOS valuation functions \(\{v_{iT}:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}_+\}_{i\in[n],\,T\subseteq[m]}\) (with no separability assumption), the expected welfare achieved by \(\mathcal{M}\) at every signal profile \(s\) is at least \(\frac{1}{c}\) times the optimal welfare at \(s\).
location_in_paper: Section 7 (problems), page 16 (in the provided PDF pagination)
open_problem_quote: For combinatorial auctions with multidimensional signals, is separability a necessary condition for achieving constant approximation to welfare? This problem is open even for single-dimensional signals and even for “simple” combinatorial valuations, such as unit demand.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks whether separability is necessary, so a counterexample would be a concrete SOS valuation family where every universally ex post IC-IR mechanism has super-constant (or unbounded) approximation ratio. Such hardness can often be explored by automated search over small instances (few agents/items, discrete signal grids) while encoding ex post IC constraints (e.g., via cycle-monotonicity) and optimizing the best achievable welfare ratio.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate hard instances (valuation tables over small discrete signal spaces satisfying SOS constraints) and compute the optimal achievable approximation ratio of mechanisms via linear/convex programs encoding ex post IC/IR feasibility, yielding an automatic fitness signal. Scaling to larger instances and ensuring SOS constraints may be computationally heavy, but iterative search could plausibly discover informative small counterexamples or suggest mechanism templates beyond separability.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Eden et al. (2023) introduce SOS (submodular-over-signals) as a substitutes-like condition on interdependent valuations and show that random partitioning of agents into “certain losers” and “potential winners” yields truthful welfare approximation because winners’ allocations are computed using proxy values that ignore other winners’ signals. The key technical step is their Lemma 2: for a d-SOS function \(v\), if one zeros out a uniformly random subset of other agents’ signals, the expected remaining value is at least \(\frac{1}{d+1}v(s)\). This underpins their constant-factor mechanisms in single-parameter environments and, under an additional separability condition \(v_{iT}(s_T)=g_{-i,T}(s_{-i,T})+h_{iT}(s_{iT})\), their RS-VCG mechanism achieving a 4-approximation for combinatorial auctions with multidimensional bundle-specific signals.

Subsequent work has made substantial progress on removing structural assumptions in restricted feasibility environments, but not yet in the full combinatorial-auction model with bundle-specific multidimensional signals. Several papers obtain constant (and sometimes tight) welfare approximations for SOS interdependent valuations in single-item or matroid single-parameter settings, improving constants (e.g., a tight factor 2 for binary signals) and developing new techniques such as candidate filtering and LP-duality “eating” processes. These advances demonstrate that separability is not inherently necessary for constant approximation in single-parameter settings, and they suggest alternative analytic frameworks (e.g., self-bounding/d-critical hierarchies) that might generalize.

However, none of the forward-citing papers provides a universally ex post IC-IR constant-factor welfare approximation for general heterogeneous combinatorial auctions with multidimensional bundle-specific signals \(\{s_{jT}\}\) under SOS valuations without separability, nor do they prove an impossibility specific to that auction model. Related negative evidence appears in the public-projects analogue, where universal truthfulness can force \(1/m\) approximations even under separable SOS unless exclusion is allowed, hinting that additional structure (beyond SOS) may be required in richer outcome spaces. At present, the separability barrier in Eden et al.’s RS-VCG payments remains the central obstacle: without separability, an agent’s report can affect the externality terms needed for VCG-style truthful payments, and new payment/estimation ideas seem necessary to obtain constant-factor welfare in the multidimensional-signal combinatorial setting.

sources.title: Constant approximation for private interdependent valuations
sources.theorem_or_result: They design the Randomized Candidate Filtering (RCF) mechanism for single-item auctions with private signals and private valuation functions. For any profile of valuation functions that are d-self-bounding over signals, RCF is EPIC-IR, polynomial-time, feasible with normalization \(\eta=2(d+1)\), and achieves welfare at least \(\frac{1}{\Theta(d)}\) of optimum (Theorem 4.1). In particular, for SOS valuations over signals: monotone SOS are 1-self-bounding and yield a \(8\ln 2\approx 5.55\)-approximation; general (possibly non-monotone) SOS are 2-self-bounding and yield a \(12\ln 2\approx 8.32\)-approximation. They also extend to \(m\) identical items with unit-demand bidders with \(O(d)\)-approximation (Theorem 5.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a universally ex post IC-IR constant-factor welfare approximation in *combinatorial auctions* with multidimensional, bundle-specific signals \(s_{jT}\) and SOS valuations \(v_{iT}(s_T)\), *without* separability. This paper achieves constant-factor approximation without separability only in much more restricted allocation environments: single-item auctions (and multi-unit with identical items and unit-demand). It does not address general combinatorial allocations over heterogeneous items nor the bundle-specific multidimensional signal model; its mechanisms and analysis rely on comparing scalar values \(v_i(s)\) and lower estimates \(v_j(s_{-i})\) in a single-parameter allocation setting. Thus it provides partial progress/techniques (randomized discretization + random tie-breaking; self-bounding hierarchy) but does not resolve the combinatorial-auction open question.

sources.title: Interdependent public projects
sources.theorem_or_result:
Main welfare-approximation results for interdependent public projects with SOS valuations:
- (Impossibility) There exist separable SOS (indeed linear) valuation functions for which no universally ex post IC-IR mechanism can guarantee better than a 
\(1/m\)-approximation to optimal welfare (Theorem 4.5).
- (Possibility with exclusion) For excludable public projects with separable SOS valuations, a Random-Sampling-VCG mechanism is universally ex post IC-IR and achieves a \(1/4\)-approximation to optimal welfare (Theorem 4.7).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether separability is necessary for constant-factor welfare approximation in multidimensional-signal combinatorial auctions under SOS valuations (without separability), with universal ex post IC-IR.

This paper does not resolve that question for combinatorial auctions. It studies a different setting—interdependent public projects (a social choice/public goods analogue)—and shows that even with separability+SOS, universal truthfulness may be too restrictive: without excludability, no universally truthful mechanism beats random choice (\(1/m\)) (Theorem 4.5). It also highlights that the known constant-factor SOS mechanisms rely on an exclusion step, and adapts the Eden et al. [23] random-sampling/exclusion idea to excludable public projects to recover a constant factor (Theorem 4.7).

Thus, relative to the auction open problem, the paper provides evidence that additional structure beyond SOS (such as exclusion, and in their positive results also separability) may be essential, but it neither gives a constant-factor mechanism for non-separable SOS auctions nor proves impossibility for auctions without separability.

sources.title: Price of anarchy of simple auctions with interdependent values
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies Bayesian Price of Anarchy (B-PoA) of simple (non-truthful) auctions under interdependent values. For multi-item unit-demand settings with multidimensional per-item signals, it defines a parameter b3-heterogeneity and a limited-information-asymmetry condition b4\cdot \mathrm{Opt}^g\ge \mathrm{Opt} (where \(\mathrm{Opt}^g\) is welfare under truncated values). It proves (Theorem 4.7) that when \(n\ge m\), for \u0003b3-heterogeneous, \(c\)-single-crossing valuations, simultaneous privatized second-price auctions have B-PoA \(O(\delta\,\max\{\u03b3,c\}^2)\) under no-overbidding. It also proves strong lower bounds: even for \u03b3=c=1 common-value unit-demand instances with \(m=n^2\), every simultaneous item-bidding mechanism has \(\mathrm{Opt}/\mathrm{Eq}=\Omega(\tilde{\log} n)\) under no-overbidding (Theorem 5.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a universally ex post IC-IR mechanism achieving a constant-factor welfare approximation for combinatorial auctions with bundle-specific multidimensional signals and SOS valuations, without separability. This paper does not design truthful (universally ex post IC-IR) mechanisms for that general combinatorial-auction model; instead it analyzes equilibrium inefficiency (PoA) of simple simultaneous item auctions, mainly for unit-demand bidders with per-item signals, under single-crossing/heterogeneity and (in the multi-item positive result) a submodularity-in-others condition plus a market information-asymmetry restriction. Thus it does not resolve whether separability is necessary for constant-factor truthful approximation under SOS. However, it is related in that it (i) works in multidimensional-signal IDV settings, (ii) uses submodularity-type assumptions over signals, and (iii) provides both positive and negative results showing additional structural/market assumptions are needed for good welfare guarantees in multi-item IDV settings.

sources.title: Auctions with interdependence and sos: improved approximation
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves that for single-item auctions with binary signals and interdependent valuations that are submodular over signals (SOS), there exists an ex post IC-IR randomized mechanism achieving a tight 2-approximation to welfare: for every signal profile \(s\in\{0,1\}^n\), \(\sum_i x_i(s)v_i(s)\ge \tfrac12\max_i v_i(s)\) (Theorem 1). The allocation rule uses only probabilities \(0\) and \(1/2\), and the approach extends to matroid single-parameter feasibility constraints (Appendix C).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a universally ex post IC-IR constant-factor welfare approximation in *combinatorial auctions* with *multidimensional bundle-specific signals* \(s_{jT}\) and SOS valuations \(v_{iT}(s_T)\), *without* the separability assumption used by Eden et al. This paper does not address that model: it studies single-item (and matroid single-parameter) environments with one binary signal per bidder (not per bundle, not multidimensional over bundles), and welfare is \(\max_i v_i(s)\) rather than combinatorial allocation over items. Thus it does not resolve whether separability is necessary in the multidimensional-signal combinatorial-auction setting. It is nonetheless related as it develops tighter truthful approximation techniques under SOS and discusses (limited) progress beyond binary signals (Appendix D), which may inform attempts in richer models.

sources.title: Better approximation for interdependent SOS valuations
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies the single-item interdependent-values setting with one-dimensional signals and SOS valuations. It gives a polynomial-time ex post IC-IR mechanism achieving welfare approximation ratio \(3.31543\) (improving the prior \(4\)-approximation), by mixing (i) a contribution-based allocation rule and (ii) a biased random-sampling mechanism; see Theorem 4.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a constant-factor welfare approximation in multidimensional-signal combinatorial auctions under SOS valuations without separability, with universal ex post IC-IR. This paper does not address multidimensional bundle-specific signals nor combinatorial allocations; it restricts to the canonical single-item auction with scalar signals \(s_i\) and valuations \(v_i(s)\). Thus it does not resolve whether separability is necessary in the multidimensional-signal combinatorial-auction model. At most, it provides improved techniques/lemmas for SOS (e.g., Lemma 2 bounding summed marginal losses) and improved approximation in a much simpler special case, which might inspire mechanisms for richer settings.

sources.title: Private interdependent valuations: New bounds for single-item auctions and matroids
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves constant-factor truthful welfare approximations for private interdependent valuations in single-parameter environments: (i) for single-item auctions with private SOS valuations, an EPIC-IR “eating mechanism” achieves a 5-approximation to optimal welfare (Theorem 3.1); (ii) for matroid feasibility constraints with private d-critical valuations, an EPIC-IR mechanism achieves the tight 
\((d+1)\)-approximation (Theorem 4.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a universally ex post IC-IR constant-factor welfare approximation for combinatorial auctions with multidimensional, bundle-specific signals and SOS valuations, without the separability assumption used in Eden et al. (2019). This citing paper does not address that multidimensional-signal combinatorial-auction model: it studies (a) single-item auctions (one item) with one signal per bidder (not bundle-specific multidimensional signals), and (b) matroid feasibility constraints over bidders (single-parameter service selection), not allocation of multiple items into bundles. While it improves SOS approximation for the single-item case (5-approx) and develops LP-duality/eating-process techniques that might be conceptually relevant, it neither removes separability in the multidimensional-signal combinatorial-auction setting nor provides a mechanism for that setting.
